claim: Lost dogs in fight-or-flight mode gravitate to wooded/covered areas for safety, but any human intrusion (foot noise, ATVs, drones) can push them out of established shelter into fatal territory.
stance_target: behavioral protocol — woods/cover as high-probability lost dog refuge requiring minimal intrusion
scope_conditions: Dogs in panic/flight mode; intrusion effect strongest in first days before dog settles
quoted_support: Most lost dogs will gravitate to wooded areas because it provides them with safe cover or shelter from things it feels are a threat. They don't take the time to say, 'Hey you are my Mom, Dad, or someone trying to help', they just run.

claim: ATV/UTV/motored vehicle sounds alert a panicked dog before the vehicle is visually close — motor-based searches in wooded areas are counterproductive for scared dogs.
stance_target: why vehicle-based searching is counterproductive for scared dogs in terrain
quoted_support: A common search method we see owners use is ATVs or side-by-sides — keep in mind that the sound from the motor of an ATV, UTV, Quad, Side-By-Side, Dirt Bike, Etc. will scare them away before you get near them.

claim: Human interference at sighting locations — 'mobbing' behavior by well-meaning people — caused a Borzoi named Princess to be perpetually displaced from Wyoming to Colorado and back, ultimately dying on highway 34 in Colorado.
stance_target: sighting-response protocol — why revealing sighting location publicly during active search harms recovery
scope_conditions: Open terrain; dog in flight mode perpetually re-triggered by crowds at sightings
quoted_support: Folks would flock to a sighting. She would flee the area of the sighting. She then traveled many miles – Cheyenne, WY to Colorado; back to Wyoming; then to Colorado again. She never settled because of human interference. She was hit on 34 in Colorado.

claim: Dogs in fight-or-flight mode tend to hunker down and rest during midday — the recommended window for minimal-intrusion food station setup is midday, entering woods no more than 10-20 feet.
stance_target: temporal behavioral pattern — midday resting phase enabling food station placement
scope_conditions: Based on Alan Beck's 'Ecology of the Stray Dog' plus MAR anecdotal evidence
quoted_support: The best time to enter the woods (usually about 10-20 feet) to set up a feeding station is in the middle of the day when research shows (from the book 'Ecology of the Stray Dog' by Alan Beck, plus anecdotal evidence) that dogs tend to hunker down in the middle of the day.

claim: A lost dog pushed out of wooded shelter by search party can travel in the opposite direction toward fatal hazards — the case of a boarding-facility escapee who moved opposite direction and was killed by a train.
stance_target: search party intrusion as cause of fatal displacement
scope_conditions: Scared dog in wooded area; multiple people on foot + ATVs + cars + drones
quoted_support: We had a dog that went missing from a boarding facility... a lot of people flocked to the area on foot, in cars, four-wheelers, and with drones. While searching the wooded area she ran into, she eventually left the area in the opposite direction and was discovered to have been hit by a train.

claim: Scent-elevation technique for food stations — spraying food scent high on tree limbs via diluted spray bottle — increases the scent carry distance and helps attract a dog from further away.
stance_target: food station setup protocol for woods searches
scope_conditions: Wooded settings; sprayed scents include tuna juice, barbecue sauce, soy sauce, diluted with water
quoted_support: Getting the scent high will carry the scent further away... These can be mixed in a spray bottle (dilute slightly with water) and spray up on tree limbs above where the feeding station is set up. You can even use a ladder.
